title: Diagnostic Disagreement as an Information-Projection Divergence: An Information-Theoretic Reading of the Quiet-Sun Temperature Ratio
abstract: The quiet-Sun coronal electron-temperature ratio $R \equiv T_\mathrm{EUV}/T_B \approx 2.4$, stable across an eight-year solar cycle, is read here as a measurement of relative entropy between two diagnostic projections of the coronal electron distribution onto the one-parameter Maxwellian family. The EUV ionization temperature is a moment-matching projection against a Bethe-type ionization kernel; the radio brightness temperature is the Rayleigh-Jeans source function of thermal bremsstrahlung. For a kappa distribution in the mean-energy convention, Fleishman & Kuznetsov (2014) give the radio-side projection in closed form as $T_B = T_\mathrm{core}$; the EUV side returns $T_\mathrm{eff}$ up to a shape-dependent correction within the Dudík et al. (2014) intensity-ratio envelope. At $κ= 2.5$ the Kullback-Leibler divergences between the true distribution and its two Maxwellian projections evaluate to $0.32$ and $1.20$ nats, and their difference satisfies $ΔD_\mathrm{KL} = (3/2)[R_0 - \ln R_0 - 1] = (3/2) d_\mathrm{IS}(T_\mathrm{eff}, T_\mathrm{core})$, where $R_0 \equiv κ/(κ- 3/2)$ is the ideal closed-form ratio and $d_\mathrm{IS}$ is the Itakura-Saito distance. The identity is offered as an analytical reference for observational systems in which two diagnostics project different moments of a common non-equilibrium distribution; the eight-year stability of $R$ expresses a stability of that projection structure.
summary: This paper provides an information-theoretic reading of the temperature discrepancy observed in the solar corona. It argues that the difference between EUV and radio temperatures is not an error, but a measurable consequence of projecting a non-equilibrium kappa-distribution onto different Maxwellian families. By establishing a mathematical link between the temperature ratio and the Itakura-Saito distance, the work shows that the observed 'gap' represents the relative entropy between different diagnostic projections. This framework provides a way to quantify the non-equilibrium nature of the plasma through standard observational diagnostics.
main_problem: Explaining the observed discrepancy between EUV and radio-derived electron temperatures in the quiet-Sun corona.
main_result: The temperature ratio is reinterpreted as a measure of relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence) between two different Maxwellian projections of a non-equilibrium kappa-distribution.
method: Information-theoretic analysis using I-projections, Bregman divergences, and the Itakura-Saito distance to link diagnostic kernels to entropy.
model_or_system: The quiet-Sun corona, modeled as a non-equilibrium plasma with an isotropic electron distribution following a kappa-distribution (D&D convention).
key_observables: Temperature ratio (R = Teuv/Tb), EUV line intensity ratios (Fe IX-XIII), and radio brightness temperature (150-450 MHz).
important_parameters: Kappa parameter (kappa = 2.5), effective temperature (Teff), and the Rayleigh-Jeans limit.
main_assumptions: The electron distribution is isotropic; the radio diagnostic operates in the Rayleigh-Jeans limit; both diagnostics sample the same plasma volume.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the diagnostic discrepancy, establishes a theoretical framework using information projection, derives analytical identities linking the temperature gap to the Itakura-Saito distance, and performs numerical evaluations against solar observations.
why_interesting: The use of information-theoretic projections (I-projections) and Bregman divergences to interpret discrepancies between different measurement kernels is highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium steady states and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
